title: Amos 7
subtitle: Locusts, Fire, and the Plumb Line

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Ministry of Amos

heb.paragraph: Amos' intercession uses the same verb (slh) used in prayers for forgiveness throughout the OT. The prophet who proclaims judgment also pleads for mercy — a tension at the heart of prophetic ministry. God relents twice (vv. 3, 6), demonstrating that divine patience is real but not unlimited.

refs.note: Abraham's intercession for Sodom — the same pattern of human plea and divine response.
Moses' intercession after the golden calf, where God also 'relented' (niham) from the disaster he planned.

notes.note: God showed Amos a locust swarm preparing to devour the land. Amos interceded: 'How can Jacob survive?' God relented. This reveals God's willingness to respond to intercessory prayer.

The second vision (fire devouring the great deep and the land) is even more severe than the first. Again Amos intercedes, and again God relents. But a pattern of relenting does not guarantee permanent reprieve.

Calvin reads the two intercessions as demonstrations of God's patience: he delays judgment to give space for repentance. But patience exhausted becomes judgment confirmed — the third vision will show that the time for intercession has ended.
The 'late plantings' (leqesh) were the spring crops that grew after the king's hay harvest. A locust swarm at this stage would destroy the remaining food supply, leaving the population with nothing.

The verb niham ('relent') is the same used of God's response to Nineveh in Jonah 3:10. The parallel is instructive: God relents when there is reason to — in Jonah, repentance; here, prophetic intercession.

Stuart reads the two vision-intercession sequences as demonstrating the prophetic function at its most fundamental: the prophet stands between God and people, mediating both judgment (downward) and intercession (upward). The appeal to Jacob's smallness (v. 2, 5) is not demographic but theological: Israel cannot survive divine wrath without divine mercy.

Andersen and Freedman note that the two relenting sequences (vv. 1-3, 4-6) create a narrative tension: if God has relented twice, will he relent again? The third vision breaks the pattern, and the answer is devastating: 'I will spare them no longer.' The literary structure builds suspense before delivering the decisive blow.

hist.context: The vision sequence (chs. 7-9) shifts the book from oracular speech to visionary experience. The first two visions follow the same pattern: God shows Amos a devastating judgment (locusts, fire), the prophet intercedes ('Sovereign LORD, forgive! How can Jacob survive? He is so small!'), and God relents. This pattern establishes that judgment is not God's first impulse — he gives multiple opportunities for repentance. But the third vision (vv. 7-9) will break the pattern.

sections.header: Verses 7-9 — The Plumb Line: No More Relenting

heb.paragraph: The meaning of ʾanak is debated. Traditionally rendered 'plumb line' (a tool for testing whether a wall is true), it may instead mean 'tin' or 'lead' — suggesting a wall made of metal that God is now tearing down. Either way, the image conveys testing and judgment: Israel has been measured and found wanting.

refs.note: 'I will make justice the measuring line and righteousness the plumb line' — Isaiah's use of the same construction imagery for divine testing.
God will stretch over Jerusalem the measuring line used against Samaria — the same imagery applied to Judah after Israel's fall.

notes.note: The plumb line vision represents God's standard of righteousness applied to Israel. The nation has been tested and found crooked. Unlike the first two visions, God now declares 'I will spare them no longer.' The time for mercy has passed.
The destruction of high places and sanctuaries targets the religious infrastructure of the northern kingdom. The sword against 'the house of Jeroboam' foreshadows the dynasty's violent end.

Calvin reads the plumb line as God's immutable standard: the law has measured Israel and found them crooked. Unlike the earlier visions where intercession succeeded, this time the sentence is final. Calvin warns against presuming on God's patience: repeated opportunities for repentance that are refused lead to irrevocable judgment.

The precise meaning of ʾanak is uncertain. Options include: (1) a plumb line (traditional), (2) tin or lead (metal), or (3) destruction (by analogy with Akkadian annaku). The image of God measuring a wall suggests testing whether Israel measures up to the divine standard.

Stuart argues that the third vision marks the irreversible turning point: God's patience is exhausted, and Amos does not even attempt intercession. The silence of the prophet where we expect a plea is more devastating than any words. Stuart connects the mention of 'the house of Jeroboam' (v. 9) to the historical assassination of Jeroboam II's son Zechariah (2 Kgs 15:8-12), fulfilled within a generation.

Andersen and Freedman read the absence of intercession as the structural climax of the vision sequence. The pattern [vision → intercession → relenting] has been established twice; its disruption in the third vision is the narrative equivalent of a broken covenant. God is no longer open to negotiation.

hist.context: The third vision breaks the pattern established by the first two. God stands beside a wall with an ʾanak in his hand and declares, 'I am setting an ʾanak among my people Israel; I will spare them no longer.' This time there is no intercession, no relenting. The high places of Isaac and the sanctuaries of Israel will be destroyed, and God will rise against the house of Jeroboam with the sword. The shift from mercy to irreversible judgment is the pivotal moment of the vision sequence.

heb.paragraph: Amaziah calls Amos a 'seer' (hozeh) rather than a 'prophet' (navi), possibly as a dismissive term implying he is a freelance diviner rather than an authorized prophet. Amos' response ('I was neither a prophet nor the son of a prophet') rejects institutional credentials in favor of direct divine calling.

refs.note: An earlier confrontation between a prophet and the altar at Bethel, under Jeroboam I — Amos' encounter echoes the same location and themes.
Jeremiah's trial for prophesying against the temple — the same pattern of institutional resistance to prophetic truth.
'We cannot help speaking about what we have seen and heard' — Peter and John's parallel claim of prophetic compulsion against institutional pressure.

notes.note: Amaziah accused Amos of conspiracy against the king. Religious establishments always resist prophetic criticism that threatens their power structure. Amaziah's report to Jeroboam was a political maneuver to silence the prophet.

Amos' response is a declaration of divine calling independent of human institutions. He had no prophetic credentials, no seminary training, no professional status. God took him directly from his flock. This validates the call of every servant God chooses, regardless of background.

Amos' oracle against Amaziah personally — his wife will become a prostitute, his children will die by the sword, his land will be divided — is one of the most severe personal judgments in prophetic literature.

Calvin highlights the Reformed principle of divine vocation: God calls whom he wills, regardless of institutional credentials. Amos had no prophetic pedigree, yet his calling was as authoritative as any prophet's. Human ordination without divine calling is empty.

The phrase 'I was neither a prophet nor the son of a prophet' (lo navi anokhi w-lo ven-navi anokhi) may be translated as present tense ('I am not') or past tense ('I was not'). The past tense reading is preferred: Amos describes his former occupation before God's call, not his current prophetic status.

Stuart reads Amaziah's actions as a combination of political opportunism and sincere loyalty to the royal establishment. The phrase 'the king's sanctuary and the temple of the kingdom' (v. 13) reveals Bethel's true nature: it is a state shrine, not a house of God. The sanctuary serves the crown, not the covenant.

Stuart argues that Amos' self-identification as a shepherd and sycamore-dresser is not false modesty but a claim to independence: he has no financial dependence on prophecy and therefore no reason to say what patrons want to hear. His oracle against Amaziah (v. 17) is the harshest personal judgment in the book, targeting the priest's family, property, and life.

Andersen and Freedman read the Amaziah episode as the dramatic center of the book. The narrative interrupts the vision sequence precisely where the stakes are highest: between the irreversible plumb-line judgment (vv. 7-9) and the fourth vision (8:1-3). The confrontation between prophet and priest embodies the book's fundamental conflict between justice and institutional power.

hist.context: This narrative interruption is the only prose passage in the book and the only scene featuring dialogue. Amaziah, the priest of Bethel, reports Amos' oracles to King Jeroboam as sedition and orders Amos to return to Judah. Amos' response is one of the great declarations of prophetic independence: 'I was neither a prophet nor the son of a prophet, but I was a shepherd, and I also took care of sycamore-fig trees. But the LORD took me from tending the flock and said, Go, prophesy to my people Israel.' The passage dramatizes the conflict between institutional religion and prophetic authority.

themes.note: Prophecy at 10: this chapter is the fullest exploration of prophetic calling, intercession, and institutional conflict in the book. Judgment at 9: the plumb line vision announces irreversible doom. Mission at 7: Amos' calling from the flock exemplifies divine commissioning.

rows.label: vv.1-6
rows.text: Two Visions, Two Intercessions, Two Relentings

rows.label: vv.7-9
rows.text: The Plumb Line: No More Relenting

rows.label: vv.10-17
rows.text: Amos vs. Amaziah: Prophet Against Priest

lit.note: Chapter 7 is structured in three movements: paired visions with intercession (vv. 1-6), the decisive third vision without intercession (vv. 7-9), and the prose narrative of confrontation (vv. 10-17). The pattern of relenting is broken, and the conflict between prophetic and institutional authority comes to a head.

hebtext.note: Amos' intercession uses the same verb (slh) used in prayers for forgiveness throughout the OT. The prophet who proclaims judgment also pleads for mercy — a tension at the heart of prophetic ministry. God relents twice (vv. 3, 6), demonstrating that divine patience is real but not unlimited.

The meaning of ʾanak is debated. Traditionally rendered 'plumb line' (a tool for testing whether a wall is true), it may instead mean 'tin' or 'lead' — suggesting a wall made of metal that God is now tearing down. Either way, the image conveys testing and judgment: Israel has been measured and found wanting.

Amaziah calls Amos a 'seer' (hozeh) rather than a 'prophet' (navi), possibly as a dismissive term implying he is a freelance diviner rather than an authorized prophet. Amos' response ('I was neither a prophet nor the son of a prophet') rejects institutional credentials in favor of direct divine calling.

ppl.name: Amaziah
ppl.text: Amaziah, the priest of Bethel, reports Amos' oracles to King Jeroboam as sedition and orders Amos to return to Judah…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Locusts, Fire, and the Plumb Line

translations.text: This is what the Sovereign Lord showed me: He was preparing swarms of locusts after the king’s share had been harvested and just as the late crops were coming up.
This is what the Lord GOD showed me: behold, he was forming locusts when the latter growth was just beginning to sprout, and behold, it was the latter growth after the king’s mowings.
And the Lord asked me, “What do you see, Amos?” “A plumb line,” I replied. Then the Lord said, “Look, I am setting a plumb line among my people Israel; I will spare them no longer.
And the LORD said to me, “Amos, what do you see?” And I said, “A plumb line.” Then the Lord said, “Behold, I am setting a plumb line in the midst of my people Israel; I will never again pass by them;
